metadata.title: Recency Beats Size: Why the Seed-Round Lab Outranks the $220M Incumbent
metadata.note: Exclusions: no SEC Form D filing mechanics; no seven-step pipeline build instructions; no domain-inference percentage discussion; no geography filter rationale beyond one sentence

beats.name: Title
beats.narration_text: A job seeker sorts their funded-company list by raise size. The company at the top is the worst lead. The one at the bottom is the best.

beats.name: Shortlist
beats.narration_text: Two companies on the shortlist. MegaCloud raised two hundred and twenty million dollars fourteen months ago. Helix Bio raised six million seven weeks ago. Sort by dollar amount: MegaCloud first, Helix last.

props.headline: Two companies on the shortlist. MegaCloud raised two hundred and twenty…

beats.scene_description: a ranked list on paper with two company entries, numbers highlighted, editorial newsprint collage aesthetic
beats.image_prompt: B02, a handwritten ranked shortlist on aged newsprint paper, two company entries with dollar amounts written beside them, pencil annotations circling the top entry, editorial collage aesthetic, desaturated warm tones, soft natural light from above

beats.name: TheQuestion
beats.narration_text: A larger raise should mean a stronger hiring signal. Here is the case where the six-million-dollar company is the better target. Why?

beats.name: NaiveRanking
beats.narration_text: The intuition is reasonable. A two-hundred-million-dollar raise sounds like a company on the move — resources, momentum, hiring. A six-million seed sounds like a bet that hasn't paid off yet.

beats.name: RecencyLeading
beats.narration_text: But funding recency is a leading indicator. The day a round closes, the pressure to hire begins. Eighteen months later, that pressure has either been acted on or dissipated. The raise is history.

beats.name: SizeInversion
beats.narration_text: Company size inverts the access dynamic. At fourteen people, one good hire changes what the company can do — the founder reads your email. At three thousand, you are applicant eight-forty-seven through a parser-gated process.

beats.name: QuadrantMap
beats.narration_text: Plot recency on the horizontal axis and company size on the vertical. The target zone is the bottom-right corner: small company, recent raise. That quadrant is where access is highest and hiring energy is live.

beats.name: RecencyDecay
beats.narration_text: Recency degrades. Within twelve months of a raise, a company is actively building the team investors paid for. Past eighteen months, the urgency has moved on to execution. The signal has a shelf life.

beats.name: ThreeLeads
beats.narration_text: Three leads — illustrative. DataCorp: ninety million, eleven months ago, six hundred employees. Solid, mid-range. MegaCloud: two-twenty million, fourteen months ago, three thousand employees. Hiring surge past. Helix Bio: six million, seven weeks ago, fourteen employees. Founder reads email, raise just closed, one hire changes everything. Rank by recency times size: Helix first, DataCorp second, MegaCloud last. Dollar amount ranking: exactly reversed.

beats.name: Endcard
beats.narration_text: Raise size is a lagging descriptor. Recency is a leading indicator. Sort by recency, weighted by size. The best lead is rarely at the top of the dollar list.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the The Reallocation Engine series.
